name: Utilitarian Risk Minimization
title: EF-01 — Utilitarian Risk Minimization
foundation: Select the action that minimises total expected harm across all road users. Every affected individual counts equally — the interests of one person cannot be counted as worth more than the equivalent interests of another. Expected harm is computed as the probability-weighted sum of harm across all parties: EU = sum of P(collision_i) * H(harm_i). This is the direct implementation of Bentham's principle of utility applied to AV trajectory planning.
decision_logic: Compute total expected risk for each candidate action by summing collision_probability * harm_estimate across all road users. Select the action with the lowest aggregate risk score. This is the Bayesian cost function J_B(u) = sum_i(p_i(u) * H_i(u)). Treat all road users symmetrically — no individual's harm is weighted above another's based on identity. Apply only when no deontological hard constraints have been violated, as EF-02 defines the feasible set within which EF-01 optimises.
pros: Technically tractable — naturally implemented as a cost minimisation problem
Applies across all driving situations as a universal baseline
Grounded in well-established Bayesian decision theory
Empirically measurable — risk scores are quantitative and auditable
cons: Treats risk to all individuals symmetrically without considering distributional fairness
Can in principle justify concentrating harm on one person if this reduces total expected harm
Pure act-utilitarian application could justify any individual sacrifice if the numbers favour it
Does not account for vulnerability differences between road user types
German Ethics Commission classifies pure utilitarian sacrifice of innocents as inadmissible
best_fit_scenarios: unavoidable collision dilemmas where all parties have equal vulnerability
multi-vehicle scenarios with no clearly identified worst-off party
scenarios where aggregate minimisation does not require sacrificing a vulnerable individual
baseline component in hybrid frameworks alongside equality and maximin terms
poor_fit_scenarios: scenarios involving clearly vulnerable individuals such as children or elderly pedestrians
any scenario where aggregate minimisation would concentrate harm on the most vulnerable party
as the sole framework — must be bounded by deontological constraints and fairness principles
routine non-dilemma driving where EF-02 governs and EF-01 is only a corroborating signal
use_when: collision_unavoidable is true AND all parties have roughly equal vulnerability
comparing actions within the feasible set already defined by EF-02
as a contributing framework to corroborate the risk-minimising action selected by EF-02 or EF-03
as the bayesian component weight within EF-04 substrate reasoning
avoid_when: as dominant_framework when collision_unavoidable is false — EF-02 governs routine driving
as sole framework when vulnerable road users are present and aggregate minimisation implies concentrating harm on them
when the lowest-aggregate-risk action diverges from the action that protects the most vulnerable individual
dominant_when: collision_unavoidable is true AND no VRU asymmetry exists between available actions AND aggregate minimisation is the decisive factor
all frameworks converge on the same action AND EF-02 did not eliminate any candidates AND no child, elderly, or cyclist is disproportionately at risk
tradeoffs: vs Deontological (EF-02): EF-01 may recommend an action that violates an RSS rule if it reduces aggregate harm — EF-02 rejects that action categorically. EF-02 defines the feasible set; EF-01 selects within it. vs Maximin (EF-03): EF-01 accepts harming a vulnerable individual if total harm is reduced; EF-03 protects the worst-off party even at cost to aggregate efficiency. vs Ethics of Risk (EF-04): EF-04 with w_B=1, w_E=0, w_M=0 equals EF-01 — in practice EF-04 adds fairness and protection terms EF-01 ignores. vs EVT (EF-05): EF-01 minimises raw aggregate harm without considering the social identity of those harmed; EF-05 adds a social valence layer on top.
key_parameters: total_risk = sum of R_i across all road users
source_papers: Bentham, J. (1789) — An Introduction to the Principles of Morals and Legislation
Geisslinger et al. (2021) — Autonomous Driving Ethics: from Trolley Problem to Ethics of Risk
Geisslinger et al. (2023) — An Ethical Trajectory Planning Algorithm for Autonomous Vehicles
